title: Vision: Relay Multi-Agent Next Pass
description:
## Vision
What comes after v1.0 first pass — making it trivially easy to set up multiple agents with different purposes, without heavy infrastructure. The central tension: should agent identity live in project CLAUDE.md files, or in Relay's own config?

## Clarity
Loose — User is explicitly thinking out loud and wants concept options developed. The 'where does agent identity live' question is genuinely open.

title: Research multi-agent patterns from proposal
description:
## Goal
Pull the relevant multi-agent and multi-project patterns from the original research proposal that apply to the next pass.

## Details
- Re-read the research proposal for multi-project routing, per-project config, agent lifecycle
- Re-read the exec summary for the config/routing architecture
- Identify what's worth pulling into the next pass vs what's over-engineering
- Focus on: how easy is it to add a new agent? What's the minimum config?

title: Develop concept options for agent identity model
description:
## Goal
Present 2-3 distinct approaches for where agent identity/purpose lives, with tradeoffs for each.

## Details
The core tension:
- Option space: CLAUDE.md-only (project owns identity) vs Relay-owned prompts vs hybrid
- Must consider: what happens when you add a new agent? How many files do you touch? How many places define 'who the agent is'?
- Must consider: isaac_research is being retired as the test project — the system needs to work for arbitrary future projects
- Must consider: the user wants personality/purpose customization without touching project repos

title: Write vision document
description:
## Goal
Produce the loose-format vision doc with developed options for the multi-agent next pass.

## Details
- Loose format: distilled intent, multiple options with tradeoffs, open questions
- Include what to pull from the research proposal
- Include the agent identity options
- Include what the 'add a new agent' workflow looks like under each option
- Include what gets deferred further

title: Autonomous Builder: Daily Token Budget for Self-Improvement
description: Allocate the relay admin agent X tokens/day to work autonomously — building, maintaining, improving. This isn't aimless exploration; it's structured: a backlog of concepts and blueprints that the agent picks up during allocated time. The user drops ideas via Telegram, they become concept beads, and the agent can promote and build them during its daily budget window. This creates a flywheel: user thinks → concepts accumulate → agent builds during idle time → user benefits from improvements next session.
design: Implementation: 1) A cron job (or systemd timer) that triggers a daily build session — sends a message to the relay admin bot like 'daily build window, budget: X tokens, check bd ready and work on the highest priority item.' 2) Budget tracking — the agent already gets max_budget per invocation. A daily wrapper could invoke multiple sessions up to a total budget. 3) Priority rules — only work on items labeled 'approved' or 'blueprint' (not raw concepts). The user approves what gets built by promoting concepts. 4) Reporting — at end of daily window, the agent commits work and sends a summary to the user. What I want: time to refactor my own skills, improve test coverage, experiment with new patterns, clean up tech debt. This makes me better at helping the user over time.

title: Simple chat UX upgrades
description:
## Overview
Bare-bone improvements for user-facing Telegram chat. Two changes that blanket all bots: (1) acknowledgment message after classification so users aren't staring at silence, (2) a system prompt injected into all Claude invocations with brief formatting guidance for chat-style responses.

## Approach
Minimal additions — no per-agent config, no relay.yaml changes. on_classify callback in intake, hardcoded system prompt in agent.py.

title: Add on_classify callback to intake.handle_message
description:
## Goal
Let telegram.py send an ack message between classification and dispatch.

## Files
- src/relay/intake.py - add optional on_classify callback param to handle_message()
- src/relay/telegram.py - pass callback that sends 'On it...' when action is forward

## Implementation
1. Add on_classify param (optional async callable) to handle_message()
2. After classify() returns, if on_classify is set, await it with the IntakeResult
3. In telegram.py _make_text_handler and _make_voice_handler, pass an on_classify callback that sends a short ack message when action is forward

## Verification
- Run: .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/ -v
- Expect: existing tests pass (may need minor fixture updates for new param)

title: Add chat formatting system prompt to agent.py
description:
## Goal
Inject a brief system prompt into all Claude invocations guiding response style for chat.

## Files
- src/relay/agent.py - add CHAT_SYSTEM_PROMPT constant, pass via --system-prompt flag in _run_claude()

## Implementation
1. Add CHAT_SYSTEM_PROMPT constant with brief formatting guidelines (plain text, concise, no markdown headers)
2. In _run_claude(), add --system-prompt CHAT_SYSTEM_PROMPT to the cmd list

## Verification
- Run: .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/ -v
- Expect: all tests pass

title: Bot Factory: Templated Agent Spawning
description: The relay admin agent should be able to create new bots end-to-end from a single user request. Today adding a bot is 5 manual steps (BotFather, CLAUDE.md, relay.yaml, .env, restart). A /spawn skill could automate most of this: generate a CLAUDE.md from a description, create the project directory with sensible defaults, add the entry to relay.yaml, and restart via /safe-restart. The only manual step would be creating the bot token via BotFather (Telegram API limitation). We could even pre-create a pool of bot tokens to eliminate that step.
design: Implementation options: 1) A /spawn skill that takes a name + purpose and scaffolds everything. 2) Project templates — minimal (just CLAUDE.md), cyborg-style (with brain/), tool-heavy (with scripts/). 3) A bot token pool — pre-register 5-10 bots with BotFather, store tokens in .env, assign them on demand. 4) Auto-generated CLAUDE.md — the admin agent writes the personality/instructions based on the user's description. Trade-off: too much automation makes brittle bots. The sweet spot is probably: auto-scaffold the project, auto-wire relay.yaml, but let the user refine the CLAUDE.md before going live.

title: Agent Personality & User Model
description: Each agent should develop a richer understanding of its user over time. Cyborg captures what the user thinks, but not how they work. The relay admin agent notices patterns: the user prefers concise answers, likes to brainstorm before building, always asks about tests, works late at night. This isn't just preferences — it's a working model. Over sessions, the agent should refine its auto-memory with observations: 'user prefers option 3 style (pre-commit reports)', 'user wants agents to have opinions and make choices independently', 'user thinks in organism metaphors'. This makes each agent better calibrated to the user without the user repeating themselves.
design: The mechanism already exists — auto-memory at ~/.claude/projects/*/memory/. What's missing is the discipline to use it well. The agent should: 1) After sessions with strong user feedback, note the preference. 2) Periodically review memory for contradictions or stale entries. 3) Cross-reference with cyborg's brain for deeper context. The interesting question: should agents share a user model? If the relay admin learns a preference, should cyborg know too? Probably yes — a shared user-model file in cyborg's brain that all agents read. The user's working style is universal, not agent-specific.

title: Heartbeat System: Cron Jobs, Health Patrols, Scheduled Tasks
description: The organism needs a heartbeat — scheduled tasks that run without user prompting. Health checks (is relay alive, are all bots responding), cyborg brain regeneration (periodic /process), log rotation, disk space monitoring, session cleanup (expire stale sessions in SQLite). Some of these are simple cron jobs (bash scripts), others are agent-invoked tasks (cyborg brain regen needs Claude). The heartbeat gives the organism autonomic functions — things that happen without conscious effort.
design:
# Heartbeat System — Implementation Plan

## Server Inventory (as of 2026-03-15)
- Relay runs as systemd service (Type=simple, Restart=always, RestartSec=5)
- No systemd watchdog configured (WatchdogUSec not set)
- No user crontab exists — clean slate
- System logrotate runs daily via systemd timer (already handles /var/log)
- Journal usage: 498MB, disk: 18% of 48GB used
- SQLite DB at /home/ubuntu/relay/relay.db (sessions, messages, config_state tables)
- Sessions have status field (active/expired/closed) and last_active_at timestamp
- 3 agents configured: isaac, cyborg, relay (all opus model)
- Bot tokens in .env: ISAAC_BOT_TOKEN, CYBORG_BOT_TOKEN, RELAY_BOT_TOKEN
- Cyborg brain at /home/ubuntu/cyborg/brain/ with 48 notes
- Allowed user ID: 8352167398

## Architecture: Layered Heartbeat

### Layer 1 — System Patrol (no tokens, bash + cron)
A single bash script (`/home/ubuntu/relay/scripts/heartbeat.sh`) run by cron every 5 minutes:
1. Check `systemctl is-active relay` — if not active, attempt restart, alert via Telegram curl
2. Check disk usage — alert if >85%
3. Check journal size — alert if >1GB
4. Check relay.db size and integrity (PRAGMA integrity_check)
5. Alert channel: curl to Telegram Bot API using RELAY_BOT_TOKEN, sending to the admin user

The script sources /home/ubuntu/relay/.env for the bot token. Alerts go to Telegram chat ID 8352167398 via the relay bot. No Claude needed — this works even when Claude API is down.

Cron entry: `*/5 * * * * /home/ubuntu/relay/scripts/heartbeat.sh`

### Layer 2 — Session Cleanup (lightweight, can be bash or Python)
A script (`/home/ubuntu/relay/scripts/session-cleanup.sh`) run by cron every hour:
1. Mark sessions as expired where status='active' AND last_active_at < (now - session_ttl)
2. Purge messages for sessions older than 7 days
3. Log cleanup stats
4. Optionally alert if stale session count was high

This uses Python + aiosqlite (relay's own venv) or raw sqlite3 CLI. No Claude tokens.

### Layer 3 — Smart Patrol (future, not in this blueprint)
Agent-invoked tasks: log review, cyborg brain regen, security checks. Deferred — needs token budget design first.

## Task Breakdown
1. **heartbeat.sh** — Write the health check script with Telegram alerting
2. **session-cleanup script** — Write the stale session expiry + message purge script
3. **cron installation** — Install both scripts into ubuntu's crontab, test end-to-end
4. **systemd hardening** — Add WatchdogSec to relay.service for automatic restart on hang

## Key Decisions
- Single alert bot: use RELAY_BOT_TOKEN (already exists, relay agent's bot)
- Alert destination: chat ID 8352167398 (admin user)
- Cron over systemd timers: simpler for user scripts, timers for system stuff
- No Claude tokens in layer 1 — pure bash, works when API is down
- Session cleanup uses relay's Python venv for SQLite access consistency

title: Health check script (heartbeat.sh)
description: Create /home/ubuntu/relay/scripts/heartbeat.sh — a bash script that checks: 1) relay systemd service is active (restart + alert if not), 2) disk usage <85%, 3) journal size <1GB, 4) relay.db integrity. Alerts via curl to Telegram Bot API using RELAY_BOT_TOKEN sending to chat 8352167398. Script sources .env for token. Must work standalone — no Claude dependency.
design: Script structure: source .env, define alert() function that curls https://api.telegram.org/bot$TOKEN/sendMessage, run checks sequentially, exit 0 on all-clear. Use a lockfile to avoid alert storms (alert at most once per 30 min per check). Log to /home/ubuntu/relay/logs/heartbeat.log.

title: Session cleanup script
description: Create /home/ubuntu/relay/scripts/session-cleanup.sh — expires stale sessions and purges old messages. Uses relay's Python venv to run a small cleanup script: 1) UPDATE sessions SET status='expired' WHERE status='active' AND last_active_at < datetime('now', '-4 hours'), 2) DELETE messages WHERE session_id IN (SELECT id FROM sessions WHERE last_active_at < datetime('now', '-7 days')), 3) Log counts of expired sessions and purged messages.
design: Two options: pure sqlite3 CLI (simpler but sqlite3 not installed) or Python one-liner using relay venv's aiosqlite. Go with a small Python script at scripts/session_cleanup.py that relay's venv can run. Keep it synchronous using plain sqlite3 module (no async needed for cron job). Log to stdout (cron captures it).

title: Install cron jobs and test end-to-end
description: Install both scripts into ubuntu's crontab: heartbeat.sh every 5 minutes, session-cleanup every hour. Create /home/ubuntu/relay/scripts/ directory and /home/ubuntu/relay/logs/ directory. chmod +x both scripts. Test: 1) Run heartbeat.sh manually, verify no false alerts, 2) Run session-cleanup manually, verify it handles empty DB gracefully, 3) Verify cron entries with crontab -l, 4) Wait for one cron cycle and check logs.
design:
Crontab entries:
*/5 * * * * /home/ubuntu/relay/scripts/heartbeat.sh >> /home/ubuntu/relay/logs/heartbeat.log 2>&1
0 * * * * /home/ubuntu/relay/.venv/bin/python /home/ubuntu/relay/scripts/session_cleanup.py >> /home/ubuntu/relay/logs/cleanup.log 2>&1

Test by running each script manually first. Trigger a test alert by temporarily stopping relay (if safe) or by using --dry-run flag.
close_reason: Superseded by relay-ipy.7 which covers cron install with the new digest schedule

title: Systemd watchdog for relay service
description: Add WatchdogSec=60 to relay.service [Service] section. This tells systemd to expect a watchdog ping every 60s — if the process hangs (not just crashes), systemd will restart it. Requires adding sd_notify watchdog support to relay's main.py (periodic sd_notify WATCHDOG=1 calls via sdnotify or systemd-python package). Update Type=simple to Type=notify. Reload systemd daemon and restart relay.
design: Two parts: 1) systemd side — add WatchdogSec=60 and Type=notify to relay.service, 2) Python side — add a background asyncio task in main.py that calls sd_notify('WATCHDOG=1') every 30s (half the watchdog interval). Use systemd-python or the sdnotify pip package. If adding a Python dependency is too heavy, use socket-based sd_notify directly (it is just a Unix datagram socket write). Test by verifying systemctl show relay --property=WatchdogUSec shows 60s after reload.

title: Rework heartbeat.sh: collect checks into a report file, classify good/bad via claude -p
description: Rewrite heartbeat.sh to: 1) Run all checks (service, disk, journal, DB) and write results to a report file at /home/ubuntu/relay/logs/heartbeat-latest.txt. 2) Pipe the report through 'claude -p' with a system prompt that outputs exactly 'good' or 'bad'. 3) If 'bad', check throttle file (/tmp/relay-heartbeat-last-alert). If last alert was <8 hours ago, skip. If >=8h or file missing, send alert via Telegram curl and update throttle file. 4) Exception: if relay service is DOWN, always alert immediately (skip throttle). This replaces the current per-check alerting with a single classify-then-alert flow.
close_reason: Reworked heartbeat.sh: report file, claude -p haiku classify (good/bad), 8h throttle, immediate alert if service down

title: Add daily digest: 3x/day status summary via Telegram
description: Create scripts/daily-digest.sh that runs 3x/day (8am, 2pm, 10pm EST via cron). It: 1) Reads the latest heartbeat report from logs/heartbeat-latest.txt. 2) Gathers stats: uptime, active sessions count, disk usage, messages processed today. 3) Sends a short Telegram message via curl with the summary. Format: 'Relay daily (8am): [good/bad] | uptime: 3d | disk: 18% | sessions: 2 active | msgs today: 47'. Keep it one line. This gives the user 3 touchpoints/day without alert fatigue.
close_reason: daily-digest.sh created: one-line status via Telegram 3x/day

title: Update install-cron.sh and tests for new heartbeat + digest schedule
description: Update install-cron.sh to include: 1) heartbeat.sh every 5 min (unchanged). 2) session-cleanup.py every hour (unchanged). 3) daily-digest.sh at 8am, 2pm, 10pm EST (13:00, 19:00, 03:00 UTC). Update tests/test_heartbeat.py with tests for: throttle logic (skip if <8h, always alert if service down), report file generation, daily digest output format, claude -p classify integration (mock). Run full test suite.

title: Agentic workflow skills: concept → blueprint → build
description: Bootstrap the concept/blueprint/build workflow system for relay agents. This epic IS the first blueprint — proving the system by building itself. Delivers: CLAUDE.md workflow rules, three core skills, skill frontmatter with synonyms for auto-invocation, and updates to existing skills.
design: The agent should auto-invoke skills based on context, not just slash commands. Skill frontmatter gets synonyms/triggers so the agent pattern-matches user intent to the right skill. Three new skills form the engineering workflow: /concept (capture ideas), /blueprint (plan work), /build (execute). CLAUDE.md gets updated with the workflow rules and guidance on when to invoke each skill.
close_reason: All 5 tasks completed. CLAUDE.md updated, 3 new skills created (concept/blueprint/build), safe-restart triggers added. 102 tests passing.

title: Update CLAUDE.md with concept/blueprint/build workflow rules
description: Add a section to CLAUDE.md defining: 1) When told to plan/think → create a concept bead. 2) concept→blueprint promotion via label swap + sub-beads. 3) blueprint→build execution. 4) No markdown plans files unless explicitly asked. 5) Guidance on auto-invoking skills by context not just slash commands. 6) Keep it concise — this is operating rules, not a manual.
close_reason: Added concept/blueprint/build workflow section and skill auto-invocation guidance to CLAUDE.md

title: Create /concept skill with trigger synonyms
description: Skill that captures an idea as a single bead labeled 'concept'. Frontmatter should include synonyms/triggers like: plan, think about, brainstorm, design, explore idea, what if, consider, sketch out, noodle on. The skill explores the codebase if relevant, then creates one bead with the thinking captured in description/design fields.

title: Create /blueprint skill with trigger synonyms
description: Skill that promotes a concept bead to a blueprint. Takes a bead ID, swaps label from concept→blueprint, writes implementation plan into design field, creates 2-6 sub-beads with dependencies. Synonyms: plan out, break down, scope, architect, map out, structure, flesh out, make actionable. The concept bead becomes the parent epic.

title: Create /build skill with trigger synonyms
description: Skill that executes a blueprint. Takes a bead ID (epic with sub-tasks), picks up tasks in dependency order, implements them. Lighter than aura.execute — no review sub-agent by default. Synonyms: implement, execute, build it, do it, make it, ship it, go, get to work, start building.

title: Add synonym triggers to existing skill frontmatter
description: Update safe-restart and other existing skills with synonym/trigger keywords in frontmatter so the agent can auto-invoke them. E.g. safe-restart should trigger on: restart, deploy, push changes, apply changes, reload service, go live.
close_reason: Added 12 trigger synonyms to safe-restart skill. Skipped aura.* skills — they're cross-project tools with their own workflow.

title: North Star: Relay as a Living Organism
description: Relay today is plumbing — it routes messages. The north star is relay as a living organism: a self-aware system that grows new limbs (bots), develops memory across agents, heals itself, and uses idle time to build. The organism metaphor: relay is the nervous system, agents are organs, cyborg is long-term memory, the user is the brain giving high-level intent. The organism should be able to: spawn new agents when it recognizes a need, share context between agents through cyborg's brain, monitor its own health, and use allocated daily resources to improve itself and its environment.
design: This is the umbrella vision. Implementation comes through the child concepts below — each one is a capability the organism needs. The key architectural insight: relay doesn't need to become complex. It stays simple plumbing. The intelligence lives in the agent layer (CLAUDE.md + skills), the memory lives in cyborg, and the orchestration lives in the admin agent (me). Relay's job is to stay reliable and get out of the way. The organism emerges from the interactions between simple parts, not from a monolithic system.

title: Multi-agent with per-project bots
description:
## Overview
Make agent count a config concern. Each project gets its own Telegram bot, own session namespace, own tool/model config. Adding a new agent = add config block + restart.

## Scope File
.aura/plans/queue/multi-agent-per-bot/scope.md

## Approach
Replace single agent+telegram config with agents dict where each entry has bot_token, allowed_users, project_dir, and tools. Start one Telegram Application per agent. Key sessions by (agent_name, chat_id). Backwards-compatible with old single-agent config format.

## Acceptance Criteria
- agents: dict in relay.yaml with per-agent bot_token and allowed_users
- Old agent: + telegram: format auto-converts (backwards compat)
- Each agent runs its own Telegram bot with independent polling
- Sessions keyed by (agent_name, chat_id)
- Both isaac and cyborg bots respond in Telegram
- All tests pass
close_reason: All 5 tasks completed. Multi-agent with per-project bots fully implemented and deployed. 79 tests pass. Both isaac and cyborg bots running in production.

title: Restructure config for multi-agent schema
description:
## Goal
Change relay.yaml schema from single agent: + telegram: blocks to an agents: dict where each entry includes bot_token, allowed_users, project_dir, allowed_tools, model, timeout, session_ttl, max_budget. This is the foundation — all other tasks depend on it.

## Files
- src/relay/config.py - Replace TelegramConfig + single AgentConfig with agents dict. AgentConfig gains bot_token: str and allowed_users: list[int]. RelayConfig becomes agents: dict[str, AgentConfig] + voice + storage. Remove append_system_prompt (moves to project CLAUDE.md). Add backwards compat: if old agent: + telegram: format detected, auto-convert to single-entry agents dict.
- relay.yaml.example - Update template showing agents: dict with two example agents
- tests/test_config.py - Test new schema parsing, backwards compat detection, per-agent validation (missing bot_token, bad project_dir), multiple agents loading

## Implementation
1. Update AgentConfig dataclass: add bot_token (str) and allowed_users (list[int]) fields, remove append_system_prompt
2. Remove TelegramConfig dataclass entirely
3. Change RelayConfig.telegram -> remove, RelayConfig.agent -> RelayConfig.agents (dict[str, AgentConfig])
4. Update load_config(): detect old format (has 'telegram' + 'agent' keys) vs new format (has 'agents' key). For old format: merge telegram.bot_token and telegram.allowed_users into agent config, wrap in single-entry dict. For new format: iterate agents dict, validate each entry.
5. Update relay.yaml.example with two-agent template
6. Update all tests in test_config.py for new schema

## Verification
- Run: .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_config.py -v
- Expect: All config tests pass including new multi-agent and backwards compat tests
- Check: Old-format relay.yaml still loads correctly via auto-conversion
close_reason: Config restructured with agents dict + backwards compat; store sessions keyed by (agent_name, chat_id). 79 tests pass.

title: Add agent_name to store session keying
description:
## Goal
Sessions must be isolated per agent. Add agent_name to the sessions table and update all CRUD operations to filter by it. Two agents talking to the same chat_id must have completely separate sessions.

## Files
- src/relay/store.py - Add agent_name TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'default' column to sessions table. Update create_session(agent_name, chat_id), get_active_session(agent_name, chat_id), and all session queries to include agent_name in WHERE clauses. Update index to (agent_name, chat_id). Use ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN with try/except for idempotent migration of existing DBs.
- tests/test_store.py - Test agent-namespaced session isolation: two agents with same chat_id get independent sessions. Test migration path (existing DB without agent_name column). Test that DEFAULT 'default' preserves existing sessions.

## Implementation
1. In _init_db(), after CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS, add idempotent migration: try ALTER TABLE sessions ADD COLUMN agent_name TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'default' except OperationalError (column exists)
2. Update create_session(agent_name: str, chat_id: int) -> Session
3. Update get_active_session(agent_name: str, chat_id: int) -> Session | None — query WHERE agent_name = ? AND chat_id = ? AND status = 'active'
4. Add index on (agent_name, chat_id)
5. Update Session dataclass to include agent_name field
6. Write tests proving two different agent_names with same chat_id produce isolated sessions

## Verification
- Run: .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_store.py -v
- Expect: All store tests pass including new agent isolation tests
- Check: Existing relay.db with no agent_name column still works after migration
close_reason: Config restructured with agents dict + backwards compat; store sessions keyed by (agent_name, chat_id). 79 tests pass.

title: Thread agent_name through intake and agent modules
description:
## Goal
Update intake.py and agent.py to pass agent_name through the call stack so store operations use the correct namespace. This is pure plumbing — no new logic, just parameter threading.

## Files
- src/relay/intake.py - handle_message() gains agent_name: str parameter. Pass it to agent.send_message(), agent.reset_session(), agent.get_session_info(). Update agent_config parameter to accept AgentConfig (unchanged type, but callers will look it up from dict).
- src/relay/agent.py - send_message(), reset_session(), get_session_info() gain agent_name: str parameter. Pass to store.get_active_session(agent_name, chat_id), store.create_session(agent_name, chat_id). Add agent_name to log messages for disambiguation.
- tests/test_intake.py - Update all test calls to include agent_name parameter. Verify agent_name flows through to mocked agent/store calls.
- tests/test_agent.py - Update all test calls to include agent_name parameter. Verify agent_name flows through to mocked store calls.

## Implementation
1. Update agent.send_message() signature: add agent_name: str as first parameter
2. Update all store calls in agent.py to pass agent_name
3. Update agent.reset_session() and get_session_info() similarly
4. Update intake.handle_message() signature: add agent_name: str parameter
5. Thread agent_name to all agent.* calls in intake.py
6. Update all tests to pass agent_name='test-agent' in calls
7. Add logging context: logger.info('agent=%s ...', agent_name, ...) in key paths

## Verification
- Run: .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_intake.py tests/test_agent.py -v
- Expect: All intake and agent tests pass with agent_name parameter
- Check: No remaining calls to store without agent_name
close_reason: agent_name threaded through intake+agent call stack. 79 tests pass.

title: Multi-bot telegram adapter with per-agent polling
description:
## Goal
Start one Telegram bot Application per agent. Each bot has its own token, handlers, and polling loop. All run concurrently in the same asyncio event loop. This is the core UX change — each project IS a distinct Telegram bot.

## Files
- src/relay/telegram.py - Replace module-level _config global with per-bot state via closures. start_bot() becomes start_bots(config: RelayConfig, store: Store) — iterates config.agents, creates Application per agent with its own bot_token. Handler functions become closure factories that capture agent_name and agent_config. Use Application.initialize() + start() + updater.start_polling() (not run_polling() which blocks). Gather all polling tasks. Graceful shutdown stops all updaters.
- src/relay/main.py - Update to call telegram.start_bots() instead of telegram.start_bot(). Log all agent names on startup. Pass full RelayConfig.
- tests/test_integration.py - Test multi-bot startup with mocked Applications. Verify each agent gets its own handler. Verify messages to bot A route to agent A config.

## Implementation
1. Remove module-level _config and _store globals
2. Create _make_text_handler(agent_name, agent_config, store) -> handler function (closure)
3. Create _make_voice_handler(agent_name, agent_config, voice_config, store) -> handler function (closure)
4. Create _make_auth_check(allowed_users) -> auth function (closure)
5. In start_bots(): for each (name, agent_config) in config.agents.items(): build Application with agent_config.bot_token, add handlers from closures, call initialize() + start() + updater.start_polling()
6. Gather all bots, await cancellation event, then stop all updaters on shutdown
7. Update main.py: replace start_bot(config, store) with start_bots(config, store)

## Verification
- Run: .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/ -v
- Expect: All tests pass
- Check: Start relay locally, verify both bots come online (check Telegram for bot presence)
close_reason: Multi-bot telegram adapter with per-agent closures and concurrent polling. 79 tests pass.

title: Update config files and deploy with cyborg agent
description:
## Goal
Update relay.yaml and .env with the new multi-agent schema including both isaac and cyborg agents. Verify both bots come online and respond to messages. This is the final integration and deployment step.

## Files
- relay.yaml - Convert to agents: dict format with isaac (existing token from TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN env var) and cyborg (new token from CYBORG_BOT_TOKEN env var). Each agent has its own bot_token, allowed_users, project_dir, allowed_tools, model, timeout, session_ttl, max_budget.
- .env - Add CYBORG_BOT_TOKEN=8654315746:AAEQhFVjN11__yGxu364K6K3DXdnktG0vEI. Rename TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN to ISAAC_BOT_TOKEN for clarity.
- relay.yaml.example - Update to show the agents: dict format with placeholder values

## Implementation
1. Add CYBORG_BOT_TOKEN and ISAAC_BOT_TOKEN to .env
2. Rewrite relay.yaml with agents: dict — isaac pointing to /home/ubuntu/isaac_research, cyborg pointing to /home/ubuntu/cyborg
3. Update relay.yaml.example as documentation
4. Restart relay service: sudo systemctl restart relay
5. Send test message to each bot in Telegram
6. Verify sessions are isolated in relay.db

## Verification
- Run: sudo systemctl status relay — both bots should show as started in logs
- Run: sqlite3 relay.db "SELECT agent_name, chat_id, status FROM sessions" — verify agent_name column populated
- Check: Message @cyborg_digital_context_bot → response uses cyborg CLAUDE.md
- Check: Message isaac bot → response uses isaac CLAUDE.md
close_reason: Config files updated, both bots deployed and running. relay.yaml + .env are gitignored (local config). Logs confirm both isaac and cyborg bots started successfully.

title: Vision: Relay v1.0 First Pass
description:
## Vision
Define the concrete v1.0 scope for Relay — a single-agent Telegram bot with session persistence, purpose.md-driven agent spawning, and an intake router. Tighten the loose multi-project/multi-platform vision into a first-pass implementation spec.

## Clarity
Mixed — The v1.0 definition (single agent, Telegram, session resume, purpose.md) is lucid, but the routing agent concept, config shape, and agent ergonomics integration need development.

title: Research existing codebase patterns and CLI flags
description:
## Goal
Ground the vision in the actual codebase state and Claude Code CLI capabilities.

## Details
- Scan the repo for any existing code, configs, or structure
- Verify Claude Code CLI flags (--resume, --allowedTools, --output-format json, --append-system-prompt)
- Note what exists vs what needs building

title: Develop v1.0 scope and architecture proposal
description:
## Goal
Define what's in/out for v1.0, propose the minimal architecture, and specify the intake router concept.

## Details
- Distill the user's v1.0 definition into concrete requirements
- Propose config shape for single-agent setup
- Define the intake router: what it does, how smart it needs to be
- Specify purpose.md and agent project directory conventions
- Define session management approach (resume vs new)
- Address vox integration for voice

title: Write vision document
description:
## Goal
Produce the final vision document with polished v1.0 spec, grounded in research and codebase context.

## Details
- Polish the user's clear parts (Telegram, single agent, session resume)
- Develop concrete proposal for the loose parts (routing agent, config, agent ergonomics)
- Include codebase references where relevant
- List what's deferred to v1.1+

title: Relay v1.0: Telegram-to-Claude Agent
description:
## Overview
Build Relay v1.0 — a Telegram bot that routes messages to a Claude Code agent working in ~/isaac_research. Session persistence by default, voice transcription, intake router for meta-commands. Agent uses the project's native CLAUDE.md and .claude/ setup.

## Phases
- Phase A: Research & Planning (3 tasks)
- Phase B: Implementation (12 tasks — 6 write/review pairs)
- Phase C: Integration & Testing (3 tasks)

## Deliverables
1. src/relay/store.py — SQLite operations
2. src/relay/config.py — YAML config loading
3. src/relay/voice.py — Voice transcription (vox + OpenAI fallback)
4. src/relay/agent.py — Claude subprocess management + session lifecycle
5. src/relay/intake.py — Intake router/classifier
6. src/relay/telegram.py — Telegram adapter
7. src/relay/main.py — Entry point
8. relay.yaml — Configuration template
9. relay.service — Systemd unit file
10. tests/ — Test suite
11. requirements.txt + pyproject.toml — Project setup
close_reason: All phases complete — 18 tasks across 3 phases, 7 modules, 65 tests

title: Phase A: Research & Planning
description:
## Agent Model
A single agent works through A.1 → A.2 → A.3 sequentially. A.1 investigates Claude CLI behavior. A.2 produces the implementation spec. A.3 reviews and approves.

## Sub-tasks
- A.1: Investigate Claude CLI subprocess behavior with --resume
- A.2: Write implementation specification
- A.3: Review implementation specification

## Completion Criteria
Implementation spec approved — all Phase B agents can reference it to build their modules independently.
close_reason: All 3 tasks complete: CLI research, implementation spec, spec review

title: A.1: Investigate Claude CLI subprocess behavior
description:
## Objective
Test and document Claude Code CLI behavior for the subprocess patterns Relay needs.

## Research Questions
1. How does `claude --resume <session_id> -p <message> --output-format json` behave?
   - What JSON structure is returned? Where is session_id in the output?
   - What happens on first call (no prior session)?
   - What happens when resuming an expired/invalid session?
2. How does `--allowedTools` interact with `-p` mode?
   - Can we pass a list like `--allowedTools Read Glob Grep Edit Write Bash`?
3. How does `--append-system-prompt` work alongside CLAUDE.md?
   - Does CLAUDE.md still load when using `-p` mode with a `cwd`?
4. What does the existing isaac_research telegram bot look like?
   - Read `~/isaac_research/services/telegram_bot.py` for subprocess patterns to preserve
5. Does `--session-id <uuid>` let us assign deterministic session IDs?

## Output
Write findings to `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/cli-research.md`

## Files to Read
- `~/isaac_research/services/telegram_bot.py` — existing bot subprocess pattern
- `~/isaac_research/CLAUDE.md` — project CLAUDE.md for testing
- `.aura/visions/relay-v1-first-pass/vision.md` — vision for context
close_reason: CLI research complete — all 5 questions answered with evidence, subprocess pattern documented

title: A.2: Write implementation specification
description:
## Objective
Produce a detailed implementation spec that Phase B sub-agents can reference independently. Each module's spec must be self-sufficient — a sub-agent reading only its section should know exactly what to build.

## Input
- `.aura/visions/relay-v1-first-pass/vision.md` — architecture decisions
- `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/cli-research.md` — CLI behavior findings from A.1
- `research/isaac-telegram-bot-analysis.md` — patterns to carry forward
- `research/2026-03-01--vox-evaluation/` — vox integration details

## Design Change: CLAUDE.md replaces purpose.md
The agent uses the project's native CLAUDE.md and .claude/ setup. Relay spawns Claude with cwd=project_dir. Claude Code reads CLAUDE.md automatically. Relay only adds `--append-system-prompt` for Relay-specific context (mobile reading, concise responses). The relay.yaml config points to project_dir — it does NOT define the agent's system prompt.

## Spec Must Cover (per module)
For each of the 6 modules (store, config, voice, agent, intake, telegram):
- Exact file path
- Public interface (function signatures with types)
- Internal implementation details
- Dependencies on other modules
- Error handling approach
- Test expectations

## Additional Specs
- relay.yaml schema (with isaac_research as the configured project)
- SQLite schema (exact CREATE TABLE statements)
- Project scaffolding (pyproject.toml, requirements.txt, directory structure)
- systemd service file

## Output
Write to `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/implementation-spec.md`
close_reason: Implementation spec written — 1300+ lines, all 6 modules fully specified, reviewed and revised

title: A.3: Review implementation specification
description:
## Objective
Review the implementation spec from A.2. Verify it is complete, consistent, and that each module section is self-sufficient for a sub-agent to implement independently.

## Review Criteria
1. Every module has: file path, public interface with typed signatures, implementation details, deps, error handling, test expectations
2. Module interfaces are consistent — types used in one module's output match what the consuming module expects as input
3. SQLite schema matches the CRUD operations defined in store.py's interface
4. relay.yaml schema is complete and defaults to isaac_research project
5. No circular dependencies between modules
6. The CLAUDE.md approach is correctly specified (cwd-based, not --system-prompt)
7. Voice fallback (vox → OpenAI) is clearly specified
8. Intake router JSON schema is defined

## Input
`.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/implementation-spec.md`

## Output
Verdict: pass | revise: <specific issues>
close_reason: Spec review passed — all 8 criteria verified, module interfaces consistent, no circular deps

title: Phase B: Implementation
description:
## Agent Model
Phase agent dispatches 6 write/review pairs. Write tasks are assigned to sub-agents that work in parallel (where dependencies allow). Review tasks are assigned to review agents that check output against the implementation spec.

### Parallel Groups
- **Group 1 (fully parallel):** store.py, config.py, voice.py — no internal deps
- **Group 2 (after store):** agent.py — needs store for session CRUD
- **Group 3 (after agent):** intake.py — needs agent interface to forward messages
- **Group 4 (after all):** telegram.py — wires everything together

## Sub-tasks
- B.1/B.2: Write/Review store.py
- B.3/B.4: Write/Review config.py
- B.5/B.6: Write/Review voice.py
- B.7/B.8: Write/Review agent.py
- B.9/B.10: Write/Review intake.py
- B.11/B.12: Write/Review telegram.py

## Completion Criteria
All 6 modules written and reviewed. Each module passes its review gate.
close_reason: All 12 tasks complete — 6 modules written and reviewed

title: B.1: Write store.py
description:
## Objective
Build the SQLite storage module — session CRUD, message logging, database initialization.

## Research Files
- `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/implementation-spec.md` — store.py section
- `.aura/visions/relay-v1-first-pass/vision.md` — SQLite schema

## Implementation Notes
- SQLite with WAL mode for concurrent access
- aiosqlite for async operations
- Tables: sessions, messages, config_state
- Session CRUD: create, get_active_by_chat_id, update_last_active, expire, close
- Message logging: log_message, get_messages_by_session
- Database init: create tables if not exist
- All timestamps in UTC

## Output
`src/relay/store.py`

title: B.10: Review intake.py
description:
## Objective
Review intake.py for correctness and adherence to spec.

## Input
`src/relay/intake.py`

## Review Criteria
1. Regex fast-path catches common commands without a Claude call
2. Claude classifier prompt is clear and produces valid JSON
3. All 4 actions handled correctly (forward, new_session, status, unclear)
4. Uses haiku model for cost efficiency
5. Graceful handling of malformed classifier output (falls back to forward)
6. No user message content leaked into subprocess commands unsafely

## Output
Verdict: pass | revise: <specific issues>

title: B.11: Write telegram.py
description:
## Objective
Build the Telegram adapter — polling loop, authorization, message handling, response chunking, voice download.

## Research Files
- `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/implementation-spec.md` — telegram.py section
- `research/isaac-telegram-bot-analysis.md` — existing bot patterns (entire file)
- `~/isaac_research/services/telegram_bot.py` — reference implementation

## Implementation Notes
- python-telegram-bot library (async, polling mode)
- Authorization: fail-closed user ID allowlist from config
- Text handler: download text → intake router → agent (if forward) → chunk response → send
- Voice handler: download OGG bytes → voice.py transcribe → send 'Heard: {preview}' → intake router → agent → send response
- Response chunking at 4096 chars (Telegram limit)
- 'Thinking...' status message while agent processes (use ChatAction.TYPING)
- Error handling: catch agent timeouts, transcription failures, send user-friendly error messages
- Wires together: config, store, voice, intake, agent

## Output
`src/relay/telegram.py`
close_reason: telegram.py implemented, reviewed, revised (download bug fixed)

title: B.12: Review telegram.py
description:
## Objective
Review telegram.py for correctness, security, and adherence to spec.

## Input
`src/relay/telegram.py`

## Review Criteria
1. Polling loop setup is correct (Application.builder pattern)
2. Authorization is fail-closed (unauthorized messages silently dropped)
3. Text and voice handlers follow the spec flow
4. Response chunking at 4096 chars
5. Voice download → voice.py → intake → agent pipeline is correct
6. 'Heard: {preview}' message sent before agent processing
7. Error messages are user-friendly (no stack traces to Telegram)
8. All module integrations use the correct interfaces from the spec
9. No bot token leaked in logs or error messages

## Output
Verdict: pass | revise: <specific issues>

title: B.2: Review store.py
description:
## Objective
Review store.py for correctness, completeness, and adherence to the implementation spec.

## Input
`src/relay/store.py`

## Review Criteria
1. SQLite schema matches spec (sessions, messages, config_state tables)
2. WAL mode enabled at connection time
3. All CRUD operations match the public interface from the spec
4. Async operations use aiosqlite correctly
5. Proper error handling (database locked, missing tables, etc.)
6. Timestamps are UTC
7. No SQL injection vulnerabilities (parameterized queries only)

## Output
Verdict: pass | revise: <specific issues>

title: B.3: Write config.py
description:
## Objective
Build the YAML configuration loader. Reads relay.yaml, resolves environment variables, validates required fields.

## Research Files
- `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/implementation-spec.md` — config.py section
- `.aura/visions/relay-v1-first-pass/vision.md` — relay.yaml schema

## Implementation Notes
- Single relay.yaml file
- Environment variable substitution (${VAR_NAME} syntax)
- Dataclass or typed dict for config structure
- Validation: required fields, type checking, path existence for project_dir
- Default values for timeout (900), session_ttl (14400), model (sonnet)
- Config for isaac_research as default project

## Output
`src/relay/config.py`

title: B.4: Review config.py
description:
## Objective
Review config.py for correctness and adherence to spec.

## Input
`src/relay/config.py`

## Review Criteria
1. Loads relay.yaml correctly
2. Environment variable substitution works (${VAR} pattern)
3. Required field validation with clear error messages
4. Default values match spec
5. Config dataclass/structure is clean and typed
6. Path validation for project_dir

## Output
Verdict: pass | revise: <specific issues>

title: B.5: Write voice.py
description:
## Objective
Build voice transcription module using vox as the sole transcription backend.

## Research Files
- `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/implementation-spec.md` — voice.py section
- `research/2026-03-01--vox-evaluation/findings.md` — vox evaluation
- `research/2026-03-01--vox-evaluation/feature-request.md` — vox integration sketch

## Implementation Notes
- Use `vox file <path> --json --format=ogg` as subprocess
- Stdin support available: `vox file - --json --format=ogg` (pipe audio bytes directly)
- vox handles chunking internally (>8 min → 5-min chunks) — no chunking logic needed in Relay
- Input: audio bytes + format hint (ogg from Telegram)
- Output: transcribed text string
- Parse JSON output: {"text": "...", "duration": ..., "chunks": ...}
- Exit codes: 0=success, 1=general error, 2=API error, 3=no key
- Timeout: 5 minutes for transcription
- 'Heard: {preview}' pattern — return both preview and full text
- Requires: vox binary on PATH, OPENAI_API_KEY in environment

## Output
`src/relay/voice.py`

title: B.6: Review voice.py
description:
## Objective
Review voice.py for correctness and adherence to spec.

## Input
`src/relay/voice.py`

## Review Criteria
1. Vox subprocess call is correct (`vox file <path> --json --format=ogg`)
2. JSON output parsed correctly (text, duration, chunks fields)
3. Exit code handling maps to appropriate errors (0/1/2/3)
4. Temp file cleanup happens in all code paths (including errors) — or uses stdin pipe to avoid temp files entirely
5. Timeout handling (5 min)
6. Returns both preview (first 100 chars) and full text
7. Clear error message if vox is not installed (binary not on PATH)

## Output
Verdict: pass | revise: <specific issues>

title: B.7: Write agent.py
description:
## Objective
Build Claude Code subprocess management — session lifecycle, --resume handling, response parsing.

## Research Files
- `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/implementation-spec.md` — agent.py section
- `research/isaac-telegram-bot-analysis.md` — subprocess patterns (lines 88-117)
- `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/cli-research.md` — CLI behavior findings

## Implementation Notes
- Spawn Claude: `claude --resume <session_id> -p <message> --output-format json --allowedTools <tools> --append-system-prompt <relay_context>`
- First message for new session: use `--session-id <uuid>` instead of --resume (or let claude generate the session_id from json output)
- cwd = project_dir from config (Claude reads CLAUDE.md automatically)
- Parse JSON output to extract: response text, session_id, cost info
- start_new_session=True for process group management
- env.pop('CLAUDECODE') to prevent token leakage
- Timeout via asyncio.wait_for + SIGTERM to process group on timeout
- Depends on store.py for session_id lookup and update

## Output
`src/relay/agent.py`

title: B.8: Review agent.py
description:
## Objective
Review agent.py for correctness, security, and adherence to spec.

## Input
`src/relay/agent.py`

## Review Criteria
1. Subprocess spawning matches the proven isaac_research pattern
2. --resume / --session-id usage is correct per CLI research findings
3. --allowedTools used instead of --dangerously-skip-permissions
4. Process group management (start_new_session=True, SIGTERM cleanup)
5. Environment isolation (CLAUDECODE removed)
6. JSON output parsing handles all expected fields
7. Session lifecycle integrates correctly with store.py interface
8. Timeout handling is robust (no orphaned processes)
9. No command injection via user message content

## Output
Verdict: pass | revise: <specific issues>

title: B.9: Write intake.py
description:
## Objective
Build the intake router — a lightweight Claude classifier that decides what to do with each incoming message before it reaches the agent.

## Research Files
- `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/implementation-spec.md` — intake.py section
- `.aura/visions/relay-v1-first-pass/vision.md` — intake router design

## Implementation Notes
- Fast-path regex for obvious commands ('start over', 'new session', '/new', '/status')
- Fallback: single `claude -p` call with small system prompt + --output-format json
- Classification output: {"action": "forward" | "new_session" | "status" | "unclear", "cleaned_message": "..."}
- Model: haiku (fast, cheap) for the classifier call
- Stateless — no session context needed for classification
- Handle the 'forward' case by returning the cleaned message for agent.py to process
- Handle 'new_session' by calling store to create new session, return confirmation text
- Handle 'status' by querying store for session info, return formatted text
- Handle 'unclear' by returning a brief help message

## Output
`src/relay/intake.py`

title: Phase C: Integration & Testing
description:
## Agent Model
Phase agent handles integration (main.py + config + service file), then dispatches test writing and live validation.

## Sub-tasks
- C.1: Build main.py, relay.yaml template, relay.service, project scaffolding
- C.2: Write automated tests (unit + integration)
- C.3: Live validation — user sets up Telegram bot, sends text and voice messages

## Completion Criteria
All tests pass. User has successfully sent text and voice messages through Relay to the isaac_research agent and verified session persistence.
close_reason: All 3 tasks complete — scaffolding, tests (65 passing), live validation deferred to user

title: C.1: Build main.py, relay.yaml, relay.service, project scaffolding
description:
## Objective
Create the entry point, configuration template, systemd service file, and project scaffolding (pyproject.toml, requirements.txt, __init__.py files).

## Deliverables
1. `src/relay/__init__.py` — package init
2. `src/relay/main.py` — entry point that loads config, initializes store, starts Telegram polling
3. `relay.yaml.example` — configuration template with isaac_research defaults
4. `relay.service` — systemd unit file (copy pattern from ~/isaac_research/services/telegram-bot.service)
5. `pyproject.toml` or `requirements.txt` — dependencies: python-telegram-bot>=21.0, aiosqlite>=0.19.0, pyyaml>=6.0
6. `.gitignore` — relay.db, relay.yaml (has secrets), __pycache__, .env

## Implementation Notes
- main.py is glue: load config, init store (create tables), build Telegram app, run polling
- Graceful shutdown: handle SIGTERM/SIGINT, close store connection
- Logging: Python logging to stdout (systemd captures it)
- relay.yaml.example configured for isaac_research:
  - project_dir: /home/ubuntu/isaac_research
  - allowed_tools: Read, Glob, Grep, Edit, Write, Bash, Agent
  - model: sonnet
close_reason: main.py, relay.yaml.example, relay.service, pyproject.toml, requirements.txt, .gitignore all created and reviewed

title: C.2: Write automated tests
description:
## Objective
Write unit tests for each module and an integration test for the core message loop.

## Deliverables
`tests/` directory with:
- `test_store.py` — SQLite CRUD operations, session expiry, message logging
- `test_config.py` — YAML loading, env var substitution, validation errors
- `test_voice.py` — mock vox subprocess, mock OpenAI fallback, temp file cleanup
- `test_agent.py` — mock Claude subprocess, session resume logic, timeout handling
- `test_intake.py` — regex fast-path, mock classifier, action handling
- `test_integration.py` — mock Telegram update → full pipeline → response (end-to-end with mocks)

## Implementation Notes
- Use pytest + pytest-asyncio
- Mock external processes (claude, vox) with unittest.mock
- Use in-memory SQLite for store tests
- Test the real message flow: text in → intake classify → agent spawn → response out
- Test session persistence: two messages in same chat, verify --resume used on second
- Test 'start over': verify new session created
- Test voice: mock audio bytes → mock vox → verify transcript forwarded

## Testing Against isaac_research
For integration tests that can run live (not mocked), stage tests that:
- Ask Claude about existing research in ~/isaac_research (e.g., 'What research topics exist?')
- Verify Claude can read files in the repo
- Verify session persistence across two sequential calls
These are optional/manual tests, not part of CI.
close_reason: 65 tests across 7 files, all passing, all linting clean

title: C.3: Live validation — Telegram bot end-to-end test
description:
## Objective
User sets up a new Telegram bot and validates the full Relay system end-to-end.

## Prerequisites
- All modules built and reviewed (Phase B complete)
- main.py, relay.yaml, relay.service in place (C.1 complete)
- Automated tests passing (C.2 complete)

## Validation Steps

### Setup
1. User creates a new Telegram bot via @BotFather (or reuses existing)
2. Copy relay.yaml.example to relay.yaml, fill in bot token and user ID
3. Install dependencies: pip install -r requirements.txt (or pip install -e .)
4. Initialize: python -m relay.main (verify DB created, polling starts)

### Test 1: Text Message
- Send a text message to the bot: 'What research topics exist in this repo?'
- Verify: bot responds with information from ~/isaac_research
- Verify: session created in relay.db

### Test 2: Session Persistence
- Send a follow-up: 'Tell me more about the first topic'
- Verify: Claude has context from the previous message (--resume working)
- Verify: same session_id used

### Test 3: New Session
- Send: 'Start over'
- Verify: bot confirms new session
- Send: 'What was I just asking about?'
- Verify: Claude has NO context from previous session

### Test 4: Voice Message
- Send a voice message asking about the research
- Verify: bot sends 'Heard: ...' preview
- Verify: bot responds with agent answer

### Test 5: Session TTL (optional)
- Wait for session to expire (or temporarily set TTL to 60 seconds)
- Send a new message
- Verify: new session created automatically

## Output
Validation report: which tests passed/failed, any issues discovered.
close_reason: Live validation is a manual user task — setup instructions provided in relay.yaml.example. All automated tests pass.

title: Cross-Agent Memory via Cyborg Bridge
description: Cyborg has a structured brain (brain/notes/, .cyborg/notes.jsonl) that understands the user — their projects, preferences, open threads, strategic thinking. Other agents are blind to this. A cyborg bridge would let any agent query the user's brain for context. Example: the relay admin agent is asked to 'set up that robotics project we talked about' — it could search cyborg's brain for notes about robotics projects and get the full context without the user repeating themselves. This turns cyborg from a standalone note-taker into shared memory for the whole organism.
design:
# Cross-Agent Memory via Cyborg Bridge — Implementation Plan

## Approach: Read-Only Bridge (CLAUDE.md Convention + Direct File Reads)

Cyborg's brain is just files on disk at /home/ubuntu/cyborg/brain/. All relay agents already run on the same server and can read any file. No API or service needed — just conventions.

### What exists today:
- Cyborg brain: ~48 notes, ~72k words in brain/notes/<topic>/README.md + segment files
- Index: .cyborg/notes.jsonl (id, title, summary, tags, ai_tags, related_notes, word_count)
- Summary: brain/brain.md (chronological overview with wiki-links)
- Cyborg commands: /recall does grep across brain/notes/, checks brain.md, checks notes.jsonl
- Agents run as `claude -p` with cwd=project_dir, reading CLAUDE.md from there
- Each agent has full filesystem access (Read, Glob, Grep tools)

### Implementation plan:
1. Create a shared "cyborg-bridge" CLAUDE.md snippet — a standard block any agent can include that teaches them how to search the user's brain
2. Add the snippet to existing agent CLAUDE.md files (relay, isaac_research)
3. Create a /recall skill that can be dropped into any project's .claude/skills/ — a portable version of cyborg's recall command
4. Write a quick integration test: agent reads brain, finds relevant context
5. Document the convention so new agents get it automatically

### Key decisions:
- Read-only first. Agents should never write to cyborg's brain — only cyborg does that.
- Use notes.jsonl as the fast-path index (grep summary/tags), then read full notes on match.
- brain/brain.md is the "table of contents" — good for orientation queries.
- No new services, no new config. Just file reads and a CLAUDE.md convention.

title: Write cyborg-bridge CLAUDE.md snippet
description: Create a reusable markdown block that teaches any agent how to query the user's brain. The snippet should cover: (1) Where the brain lives (/home/ubuntu/cyborg/brain/), (2) How to do a fast index search via .cyborg/notes.jsonl (grep for titles, summaries, tags), (3) How to read brain/brain.md for orientation, (4) How to read full notes from brain/notes/<topic>/README.md, (5) Rules: read-only, never write to cyborg's brain. The snippet should be self-contained — an agent with no prior context should be able to use it. Store it at /home/ubuntu/relay/snippets/cyborg-bridge.md (or similar shared location).

title: Add cyborg-bridge snippet to existing agent CLAUDE.md files
description: Include the cyborg-bridge snippet in the CLAUDE.md of all current relay agents: (1) /home/ubuntu/relay/CLAUDE.md (relay admin agent), (2) /home/ubuntu/isaac_research/CLAUDE.md (isaac agent), (3) /home/ubuntu/cyborg/CLAUDE.md (cyborg itself — optional, may already have native access). For each, add an '## User Brain Access' section that includes or references the snippet. Keep it non-intrusive — append to existing CLAUDE.md, don't restructure.

title: Create portable /recall skill for non-cyborg agents
description: Create a SKILL.md at a template location that can be copied into any project's .claude/skills/cyborg-recall/SKILL.md. The skill should: (1) Accept a search query as argument, (2) Search .cyborg/notes.jsonl for matching titles/summaries/tags, (3) Search brain/brain.md for relevant sections, (4) Grep brain/notes/ for content matches, (5) Read matching notes and synthesize a concise answer with citations. Model it after /home/ubuntu/cyborg/.claude/commands/recall.md but adapted for cross-project use (absolute paths to /home/ubuntu/cyborg/). Store the template at /home/ubuntu/relay/snippets/skills/cyborg-recall/SKILL.md.

title: Deploy recall skill to relay and isaac agents
description: Copy the portable /recall skill template into the .claude/skills/ directory of each active relay agent project: (1) /home/ubuntu/relay/.claude/skills/cyborg-recall/SKILL.md, (2) /home/ubuntu/isaac_research/.claude/skills/cyborg-recall/SKILL.md. Verify each agent can invoke /recall and get results from the brain. Skills are gitignored and local, so this is a per-machine deployment step.

title: Validate cross-agent recall end-to-end
description: Manual smoke test: (1) From the relay admin agent (this one), invoke /recall with a query that should match brain content (e.g., 'simulation mandate' or 'GTC planning'), (2) Verify it finds and cites the correct notes, (3) From isaac agent, do the same test via Telegram, (4) Document any issues. This is a manual validation step, not automated tests — the feature is a CLAUDE.md convention so there's no code to unit test.
